text.title: Solution Options Scoring
text.summary: Weighted multi-criteria scoring rules: options identification, criteria weighting, anchored scoring rubric, sensitivity analysis, and recommendation constraints.

rule.statement: Always include status-quo (do-nothing) as a numbered option in the scoring matrix and weighted totals; never omit it.
rule.rationale: Without status-quo as Option A, leadership cannot see the cost of inaction; the recommendation reads as advocacy rather than analysis.

rule.statement: Generate 3-7 alternatives before scoring; do not begin scoring with a single option and a strawman.
rule.rationale: Single-option matrices are decorative; sponsors recognize them as advocacy and the technique gets discredited for the next real decision.

rule.statement: Lock criteria weights before options are scored, in a separate step with named stakeholders; never let the scoring agent set weights.
rule.rationale: Weights drift to make the math agree with the pre-decided recommendation. The weight-setting step must be documented separately so the rationale survives later challenge.

rule.statement: Treat any weight sum that does not equal 100% as an error; do not silently normalize.
rule.rationale: Silent normalization hides input errors and changes the effective weight of every criterion without the stakeholder knowing.

rule.statement: Cap the criterion list at 5-7; more criteria collapse to ties and double-counting.
rule.rationale: Above 7 criteria, marginal criteria carry less than 3% weight — they add noise, not signal, and often encode the same underlying dimension twice.

antipattern.description: Criteria that partially overlap (e.g., Cost, Time to value, and Risk all encoding capital exposure).
antipattern.reason: Double-counting inflates one dimension and makes the matrix systematically biased toward options that score well on capital risk, even when that was not the intent.

rule.statement: Anchor every score on the 1-5 rubric to a measurable definition before scoring begins; free-form 1-5 produces clustering at 3-4.
rule.rationale: Without anchors, every option scores 3 or 4 on every criterion; differences wash out and the matrix produces no ranking signal.

example.note: Anchored rubric for "Strategic fit": 5 = exceeds strategic target by >=20%, 4 = meets target, 3 = neutral/unclear, 2 = partial misalignment, 1 = direct conflict with strategy.

rule.statement: Require at least one evidence_url per (option, criterion) cell; mark any cell without evidence confidence=low and force score to 3.
rule.rationale: LLMs cluster scores at 3-4 and will hallucinate vendor pricing or TCO numbers that "feel right." Evidence anchors prevent fabrication.

section.title: Sensitivity Analysis

rule.statement: Run ±10% and ±25% weight perturbation and single-criterion-removal on every published matrix; if the winner flips under <=10% perturbation, set brittleness=high and require human review before publishing.
rule.rationale: A recommendation that flips on a 10% weight wobble is statistically a coin-flip; publishing it without the brittleness flag misleads sponsors.

section.reference: Python script for weight perturbation and brittleness detection.
Solution options analysis with criteria, scoring matrix, and recommendation.
